meta.title: Lake Mead Water Elevation End-of-2027
meta.question: What will Lake Mead water surface elevation (feet above sea level) be on December 31, 2027?
meta.resolution: USBR official daily reservoir elevation reading for Lake Mead for December 31, 2027, as published on the Bureau of Reclamation Lower Colorado Region daily reservoir data portal. Fallback (in order): December 30 reading if Dec 31 is missing; SNWA published Lake Mead intake elevation for the same date; Western Area Power Administration Hoover Dam daily operations report. Reading is the end-of-day elevation in feet above sea level.

tagConfig._docs: Tag configuration for this topic. topicType presets: conflict, science, election, tech. Override directionHints for custom tag-to-hypothesis mappings.

directionHints.DATA: Check elevation readings and snowpack measurements

H1.label: Recovery: above 1,075 ft (exits Tier 1 shortage zone)

H2.label: Stable decline: 1,050-1,075 ft (remains in Tier 1 range)

posteriorHistory.note: Initial priors. Lake Mead at ~1,061 ft (35% capacity, 20 ft above record low). BOR 24-month study projects possible record low (~1,032 ft) by Nov 2027. Very low 2025-26 snowpack. H3 (0.45): favored by BOR most-probable projection landing in 1,025-1,050 range. H2 (0.32): current-level inertia + conservation measures could hold elevation above 1,050 — requires near-average snowpack. H1 (0.08): would require above-average snowpack for 2 consecutive years to recover 14+ ft — historically rare during megadrought (occurred once since 2000, in 2023 winter). H4 (0.15): BOR CRSS model 10th-percentile exceedance runs show ~12-18% probability of sub-1,025 by end of 2027 depending on hydrology assumptions; 0.15 is consistent with the pessimistic-but-not-extreme tail.
Indicator t3_monthly_drop_6ft fired (firing #1, decay=1.000): REPLAY: Fox5 Vegas reports Lake Mead dropped >6ft since March 1. USBR confirms 1,064.16

redTeam.challenge: No significant shifts proposed; no challenge needed.

tier1_critical.desc: USBR reading shows Lake Mead below 1,040.94 ft (2022 record low).
USBR reading shows Lake Mead below 1,025 ft (Tier 3 shortage threshold).
USBR reading shows Lake Mead above 1,075 ft (Tier 1 exit threshold).

tier2_strong.desc: NRCS SNOTEL Upper Colorado Basin snowpack falls below 50% of median at April 1 measurement.
NRCS SNOTEL Upper Colorado Basin snowpack exceeds 100% of median at April 1 measurement.
tier2_strong.lr_migration_warning: design prior drift >30% — review before next firing

tier2_strong.desc: BOR 24-month study projects Lake Mead below 1,040 ft at any point within study window.
Lake Powell elevation drops below 3,500 ft (minimum power pool), reducing downstream releases to Mead.
NRCS SNOTEL Upper Colorado Basin snowpack exceeds 120% of median at any monthly measurement during Nov-Feb accumulation season.
BOR 24-month study projects Lake Mead below 1,025 ft at any point within study window.
tier2_strong.lr_migration_warning: design prior drift >30% — review before next firing

tier3_suggestive.desc: Lake Mead drops more than 6 ft in any single calendar month.
tier3_suggestive.note: REPLAY: Fox5 Vegas reports Lake Mead dropped >6ft since March 1. USBR confirms 1,064.16

tier3_suggestive.desc: SNWA or NPS publishes a public advisory, board resolution, or regulatory filing citing Lake Mead water temperature as a factor affecting water treatment operations or Hoover Dam turbine cooling.

NPS issues harmful algal bloom advisories for 3 or more named Lake Mead National Recreation Area locations (e.g., Boulder Beach, Callville Bay, Government Wash, Las Vegas Bay, Echo Bay) in the same NPS advisory or within the same 7-day window.
Any Lower Basin state enacts new mandatory water conservation measures beyond existing requirements.

anti_indicators.desc: Lake Mead end-of-month USBR elevation reading is lower than the start-of-month reading for two consecutive months during snowmelt season (Apr-Jul). Measured as: last daily reading of month N minus first daily reading of month N.
anti_indicators.lr_migration_warning: design prior drift >30% — review before next firing

anti_indicators.desc: BOR 24-month study projects elevation below 1,050 ft by end of 2027.
Lake Mead elevation rises more than 5 ft in a single calendar month, as measured by USBR daily readings (month-start vs month-end).
Combined Colorado River system reservoir storage (Powell + Mead + Flaming Gorge + Navajo + Blue Mesa) rises above 40% of total capacity as reported in the BOR monthly operations summary.

actorModel.description: Institutional actors managing allocation within a physically driven system. Climate/snowpack is the primary driver (see physicalDrivers); actors manage the policy response but cannot override hydrology.

bor.name: Bureau of Reclamation
bor.role: Operates Glen Canyon and Hoover dams, sets release schedules, publishes 24-month studies, declares shortage tiers
bor.decisionStyle: Institutional/technocratic — follows hydrological models and legal framework (Law of the River)
biases: Model-dependent: projections anchor on recent hydrology
Institutional inertia: slow to deviate from established protocols
filters: Bound by Law of the River, DCP, 2007 Interim Guidelines
Must balance power generation vs water delivery
overrides: Emergency conditions (e.g., dam safety) override normal operations

snwa.name: Southern Nevada Water Authority
snwa.role: Las Vegas metro water supply; operates low-level pumping station (Intake 3)
snwa.decisionStyle: Risk-aware, engineering-first — invested in deep infrastructure
biases: May downplay severity given infrastructure investments
filters: Dependent on Lake Mead elevation for intake viability
overrides: Intake 3 can pump to dead pool — reduces urgency relative to other users

cap.name: Central Arizona Project
cap.role: Largest AZ water delivery system; junior priority rights (first cut in shortage)
cap.decisionStyle: Politically exposed, vocal advocate for maintaining allocations
biases: Lobbies to delay or soften shortage declarations
filters: Junior priority = absorbs cuts first under DCP
overrides: Severe shortage forces fallback to groundwater and alternative supplies

iid.name: Imperial Irrigation District
iid.role: Largest single Colorado River rights holder (3.1M AF/yr), senior priority
iid.decisionStyle: Protects senior rights aggressively; resists voluntary conservation
biases: Status quo bias: senior rights mean they are last cut
filters: Senior priority rights insulate from early shortage tiers
Agricultural economy dependent on full allocation
overrides: Federal intervention could override priority system

ucrc.name: Upper Colorado River Commission (CO, WY, UT, NM)
ucrc.role: Manages Upper Basin compact compliance; controls consumptive use that determines how much water reaches Powell and then Mead
ucrc.decisionStyle: Compact-driven, defensive — must deliver 7.5 MAF/yr to Lower Basin under 1922 Compact
biases: Climate is shrinking available supply faster than consumption reductions
filters: State water law and prior appropriation
overrides: Federal curtailment if compact deliveries are missed

mexico_ibwc.name: Mexico (CONAGUA) / International Boundary and Water Commission
mexico_ibwc.role: Mexico holds 1944 Treaty rights to 1.5 MAF/yr; IBWC manages binational water accounting; shortage sharing under Minute 323
mexico_ibwc.decisionStyle: Treaty-bound, diplomatic — proportional cuts under binational agreements
biases: Limited leverage but international dimension adds complexity
filters: Treaty obligations are senior to domestic DCP
Diplomatic channel separate from domestic politics
overrides: Severe crisis could prompt treaty renegotiation

methodology: ACTIONS OVER RHETORIC — only USBR data and official declarations move posteriors
TAG EVERYTHING — separate policy statements from hydrological data
SNOWPACK IS LEADING — April 1 snowpack is the single best predictor of summer inflow
PHYSICAL OVER POLITICAL — conservation measures slow decline but cannot reverse absent inflow

climate_hydrology.name: Climate/Hydrology
climate_hydrology.description: Snowpack, precipitation, evaporation — the physical driver of inflow
trends: Multi-decadal aridification trend (Colorado flow declining ~10% per decade)
exceptions: Exceptional wet years can temporarily reverse trends (e.g., 2023)

evidenceLog.text: Lake Mead dropped approximately 6 ft since March 1, 2026, now sitting 20 ft above the all-time low. Currently at approximately 35% capacity. SNWA outreach manager Bronson Mack stated the lake could drop another 16 ft by 2027. Federal projections released in March 2026 indicate Lake Mead could reach record-low water levels (~1,032 ft) by November 2027. Previous record low was approximately 1,040.94 ft, set in 2022. Very low snowpack cited as primary driver. Rising water temperatures reported as affecting water treatment operations. NPS issued harmful algal bloom advisory for Government Wash on March 13.
evidenceLog.source: The Independent (citing FOX 5 Vegas, Nevada Current, NPS)
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. Multiple indicators are directionally relevant but none have fired: t1_below_record requires USBR reading below 1,040.94 ft (current ~1,041 ft, not yet breached). t2_snowpack_below_50 requires April 1 SNOTEL measurement (described as very low but no numeric). t3_water_temp_alert requires formal SNWA advisory (concerns cited but no formal advisory). t3_algal_bloom_multi requires 3+ named locations (only Government Wash). H3-directional given BOR projection of ~1,032 ft, but no threshold crossed.
evidenceLog.note: Seed evidence. Source: The Independent (0.50 initial trust), citing FOX 5 Vegas local reporting and Nevada Current.

evidenceLog.text: Lake Mead water surface elevation at 1,064.16 ft as of April 6 (USBR weekly update). Lake has dropped more than 6 feet since March 1, now just 20 feet above the all-time record low of 1,040.94 ft from July 2022. Federal projections show Lake Mead could reach new record low of ~1,032 ft by November 2027.
evidenceLog.source: USBR Lower Colorado Weekly Hydrologic Update / Fox5 Vegas
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: t3_monthly_drop_6ft FIRED. Applied pre-committed effect: H3 +3pp, H4 +2pp, H1 -2pp, H2 -3pp. New posteriors: H1=0.06, H2=0.29, H3=0.48, H4=0.17. Sum=1.00.
evidenceLog.note: 1,032ft projection for Nov 2027 is approaching t1_below_record (1,040.94 ft) and t2_bor_projects_below_1040 territory. Monitor May 24-month study.

evidenceLog.text: Colorado Basin April 1 snowpack at historically record low levels, roughly five times lower than previous record. Federal Bureau of Reclamation projects Lake Mead will reach new record-low water levels by November 2027, potentially dropping to approximately 1032 feet.
evidenceLog.note: Matches indicator t2_snowpack_above_100 (tier2 strong). Pre-committed effect: H1 +8pp, H2 +4pp, H3 -8pp, H4 -4pp. Source trust: 50% (moderate).

evidenceLog.text: GOVERNANCE WARNING on bayesian_update: confidence_inflation, quorum_failure. Reason: Evidence ev_003: Colorado Basin April 1 snowpack at historically record low levels, roughly five

Lake Mead at ~34% capacity. Upper Basin snowpack near lowest levels during 2000-present drought. Apr-Jul inflow to Powell projected at one-third of normal. Mead was 1,062 ft in Jan 2026 (6.5 ft above projections). Post-2026 operating agreements under negotiation.

GOVERNANCE WARNING on bayesian_update: confidence_inflation, quorum_failure. Reason: Very low snowpack and one-third normal inflow projection strongly favor continued decline into Tier 2 range (H3). Small Jan buffer unlikely to persist through summer drawdown. H4 risk elevated by drought severity.
Lake Mead elevation at 1,064.16 ft in early April 2026, reservoir 34% full (~8.8 MAF). Reclamation projects year-end elevation of 1,055.88 ft, keeping basin in Tier 1 Shortage Condition for 2026.
EPISTEMIC CLAMP: posteriors clamped to [0.005, 0.98] range with redistribution — topic is ACTIVE, certainty requires resolution. Bounds enforced strictly via adjustable-mass redistribution.

Lake Mead elevation 1,064.16 ft per USBR Lower Colorado weekly hydrologic update April 6, 2026. Reservoir approximately 34% full (8.8 MAF). April 2026 unregulated inflow forecast for Lake Powell: 0.550 MAF (61% of 30-year average). Projected to remain in Tier 1 shortage with end-year elevation approximately 1,055.88 ft.
EPISTEMIC CLAMP: posteriors clamped to [0.005, 0.98] range with redistribution — topic is ACTIVE, certainty requires resolution. Bounds enforced strictly via adjustable-mass redistribution.
USBR Apr 17, 2026 24-Month Study: Lake Mead projected at ~1,038 ft by end of June 2027 under most-probable scenario; worst-case ~1,026 ft (1 ft above Tier 3 threshold).
EPISTEMIC CLAMP: posteriors clamped to [0.005, 0.98] range with redistribution — topic is ACTIVE, certainty requires resolution. Bounds enforced strictly via adjustable-mass redistribution.
LR MIGRATION RESET: posteriors reset to design priors; evidence log preserved.
RESOLUTION-DATE STAMPED: meta.resolutionDate=2027-12-31 (derived from meta.resolution). When past, governance flags topic as overdue for resolution.
REPLAY BEGIN: 1 previously-fired indicators will be re-applied chronologically under current LR system. Posteriors reset to design priors; indicator state cleared. Evidence log preserved for audit.
REPLAY: indicator t3_monthly_drop_6ft fires at historical firedDate 2026-04-10. Original note: Fox5 Vegas reports Lake Mead dropped >6ft since March 1. USBR confirms 1,064.16 ft on April 6.
REPLAY COMPLETE: 1 indicators applied, 0 skipped. Final posteriors: {'H1': 0.06, 'H2': 0.29, 'H3': 0.48, 'H4': 0.17}. Indicator firedDates restored to originals for audit.

upper_colorado_snowpack.label: Upper Colorado Basin Snowpack (% of median)

searchQueries: Bureau of Reclamation 24-month study Colorado River {date}

dependencies._docs: Cross-topic dependency declarations. Upstream topics whose posteriors this topic's assumptions depend on. Checked mechanically on save — stale assumptions surface as governance issues.

predictionScoring._docs: Calibration infrastructure. Snapshots are auto-created by engine on each posterior update. Record outcome when topic resolves, then compute_calibration_report() for Brier analysis.

resolved.entry_a_text: Colorado Basin April 1 snowpack at historically record low levels, roughly five times lower than previous record. Federal Bureau of Reclamation projects Lake Mead will reach new record-low water levels by November 2027, potentially dropping to approximately 1032 feet.

resolved.entry_a_text: Lake Mead water surface elevation at 1,064.16 ft as of April 6 (USBR weekly update). Lake has dropped more than 6 feet since March 1, now just 20 feet above the all-time record low of 1,040.94 ft from July 2022. Federal projections show Lake Mead could reach new record low of ~1,032 ft by November 2027.

resolved.entry_a_text: Lake Mead dropped approximately 6 ft since March 1, 2026, now sitting 20 ft above the all-time low. Currently at approximately 35% capacity. SNWA outreach manager Bronson Mack stated the lake could drop another 16 ft by 2027. Federal projections released in March 2026 indicate Lake Mead could reach record-low water levels (~1,032 ft) by November 2027. Previous record low was approximately 1,040.94 ft, set in 2022. Very low snowpack cited as primary driver. Rising water temperatures reported as affecting water treatment operations. NPS issued harmful algal bloom advisory for Government Wash on March 13.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H2 (Mead 1,050-1,075 ft, Tier 1), THEN BOR announces continued Tier 1 shortage operations for 2028 water year
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: BOR official announcement specifies Tier 1 (not Tier 2) shortage declaration for water year 2028

AMBER.reasoning: BOR announces Tier 1 given Tier 1 elevation is a near-definitional institutional outcome.

BLUE.reasoning: BOR Tier 1 continuation is institutional resolution within the existing framework.

RED.reasoning: BOR Tier 1 continuation under H2 is mechanically required. OK.

VIOLET.reasoning: Names at least one specific decision-maker controlling the outcome; incentive is implicit but traceable.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H2, THEN monthly average inflow to Lake Mead for 2027 exceeds 800,000 acre-feet
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: USGS/BOR monthly streamflow cumulates >800k acre-feet for Jan 1-Dec 31 2027

AMBER.reasoning: Inflow volume is physical and well-suited to smooth-trend estimation.

RED.reasoning: 800 kAF/mo inflow is a narrow hydrological assumption. RED: climate variance makes this a coin flip masked as 0.68.

VIOLET.reasoning: Pure hydrological/physical aggregate (precipitation, snowmelt, runoff, streamflow, aquifer). No named decision-maker controls the measured variable; VIOLET lens offers no actor-incentive leverage.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H2, THEN Hoover Dam power generation capacity does not fall below 70% for >2 consecutive months in 2027
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Monthly BOR reports show capacity utilization ≥70% for ≥10 of 12 months in 2027

AMBER.reasoning: Hoover capacity given elevation band is a near-mechanical relationship.

BLUE.reasoning: Hoover capacity staying above threshold reflects operational management (institutional absorption).

RED.reasoning: Hoover capacity >=70% for 10 of 12 months captures infrastructure brittleness threshold. Good.

VIOLET.reasoning: Names multiple specific decision-makers (agencies, officials, firms, states) and ties the outcome to their choices. Actor-incentive traceability strong.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H3 (Mead 1,025-1,050 ft, Tier 2), THEN BOR announces Tier 2 shortage operations mandatory for 2028 water year
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: BOR official announcement specifies Tier 2 (or lower) shortage declaration for water year 2028

AMBER.reasoning: Tier 2 declaration given Tier 2 elevation is the prescribed institutional regime change.

BLUE.reasoning: Tier 2 declaration is institutional escalation within the existing framework; not breakdown.

RED.reasoning: Tier 2 declaration under Tier 2 elevations is mechanically enforced. OK with vetter's MODIFY.

VIOLET.reasoning: Names at least one specific decision-maker controlling the outcome; incentive is implicit but traceable.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H3, THEN Arizona enforces mandatory agricultural water cuts (10-20% beyond 2026 baseline) for 2028
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: ADWR issues formal order reducing agricultural allocations 10-20% from Tier 1 baseline for water year 2028

AMBER.reasoning: Mandatory ag cuts under Tier 2 capture the forced institutional discontinuity.

BLUE.reasoning: ADWR formal order is institutional absorption of shortage.

RED.reasoning: AZ 10-20% ag cuts under Tier 2 is the correct institutional fire response. Good.

VIOLET.reasoning: Names at least one specific decision-maker controlling the outcome; incentive is implicit but traceable.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H3, THEN Lake Mead elevation falls below 1,040 feet at some point during 2027
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: USGS real-time records show elevation <1,040 ft on ≥1 day between Jan 1 and Aug 15, 2027

AMBER.reasoning: Sub-1040 ft elevation excursion is a physical threshold, smooth-trend consistent with H3 framing.

BLUE.reasoning: Elevation below threshold is pure physical outcome.

RED.reasoning: Mead <1,040 ft during 2027 under Tier 2 pathway. Mechanically consistent. OK.

VIOLET.reasoning: Reservoir elevation / dam generation read-out is a physical aggregate. VIOLET lens neutral: no single decision-maker controls the gauge reading.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H3, THEN Nevada limits Lake Mead groundwater discharge permits for ≥1 municipal water district in southern Nevada
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: NDWR denies or significantly reduces (>15%) a groundwater discharge permit application from Clark County or nearby district

AMBER.reasoning: Nevada groundwater-permit denial under Tier 2 is the institutional spillover capturing regime tightening.

BLUE.reasoning: NDWR permit restriction is a regulatory absorption mechanism.

GRAY.reasoning: NV groundwater permit denial is a sharp specific trigger.

RED.reasoning: NV groundwater permit restriction under Tier 2 is a second-order institutional action. Good â€” captures cascading stress.

VIOLET.reasoning: Names multiple specific decision-makers (agencies, officials, firms, states) and ties the outcome to their choices. Actor-incentive traceability strong.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H2 (Mead 1,050-1,075 ft, Tier 1), THEN Colorado River basin precipitation anomaly for WY2027 reaches +15% to +25% above 1981-2010 mean
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: NRCS/USGS basin-wide cumulative precipitation Oct 2026-Sep 2027 at +15% to +25% vs 30-year climatological mean

AMBER.reasoning: Precipitation anomaly is a meteorological smooth-trend outcome; framing is physical not institutional.

BLUE.reasoning: Precipitation anomaly is a pure physical outcome.

RED.reasoning: Precipitation anomaly +15-25% above mean to sustain Tier 1 is a straight climatological optimism bet. RED: SW drought regime is the base rate; this is wishful.

VIOLET.reasoning: Pure hydrological/physical aggregate (precipitation, snowmelt, runoff, streamflow, aquifer). No named decision-maker controls the measured variable; VIOLET lens offers no actor-incentive leverage.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H2, THEN municipal water demand in Las Vegas metropolitan area does NOT exceed 2024 levels by more than 3% YoY in 2027
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Southern Nevada Water Authority reports 2027 total water deliveries within 103% of 2024 total demand

AMBER.reasoning: Las Vegas demand staying within 3% of baseline is consistent with SNWA conservation regime already in place.

BLUE.reasoning: Municipal demand flat reflects SNWA's conservation-institution absorption of stress.

RED.reasoning: Las Vegas demand within 103% of 2024 is a reasonable institutional-restraint indicator given conservation regs. OK.

VIOLET.reasoning: Names multiple specific decision-makers (agencies, officials, firms, states) and ties the outcome to their choices. Actor-incentive traceability strong.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H3 (Mead 1,025-1,050 ft, Tier 2), THEN Colorado River basin precipitation for WY2027 remains 5-15% below 1981-2010 mean
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: NRCS/USGS basin-wide precipitation Oct 2026-Sep 2027 at -5% to -15% vs climatological mean

AMBER.reasoning: Precipitation band under Tier 2 elevation is a physical consistency claim.

RED.reasoning: Precip 5-15% below mean driving Tier 2 is hydrology-consistent and captures the drought-regime base case. Good.

VIOLET.reasoning: Pure hydrological/physical aggregate (precipitation, snowmelt, runoff, streamflow, aquifer). No named decision-maker controls the measured variable; VIOLET lens offers no actor-incentive leverage.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H3, THEN agricultural water allocations show structural demand consolidation: >10% reduction in active water rights holders in AZ+NV+CA tri-state region by Dec 2027
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Agricultural census or ADWR data shows >10% reduction in number of active agricultural water rights holders in tri-state region by Dec 2027

AMBER.reasoning: >10% reduction in active water-rights holders is structural consolidation, a regime-shift outcome.

BLUE.reasoning: Consolidation of water-rights holders via markets/retirement is absorption through water-market mechanisms.

GRAY.reasoning: Water-rights-holder reduction is a sharp structural test.

RED.reasoning: 10%+ ag water-rights holder consolidation is a structural-loss indicator. Strong.

VIOLET.reasoning: Names at least one specific decision-maker controlling the outcome; incentive is implicit but traceable.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H3, THEN pumping depth in western Arizona groundwater basins increases 50-150 feet as farmers respond to surface allocation cuts
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: USGS water level monitoring wells in Harquahala and Avra basins show average drawdown increase of 50+ feet between Jan 2027 and Dec 2027

AMBER.reasoning: 50-150 ft groundwater drawdown captures aquifer-regime cascade from surface cuts.

BLUE.reasoning: Pumping depth is pure physical hydrological response.

RED.reasoning: Groundwater drawdown 50-150 ft in western AZ basins captures the exact compensatory-pumping failure mode missed in 266. Good.

VIOLET.reasoning: Pure hydrological/physical aggregate (precipitation, snowmelt, runoff, streamflow, aquifer). No named decision-maker controls the measured variable; VIOLET lens offers no actor-incentive leverage.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H3, THEN California reduces Colorado River intake below 4.4 MAF QSA baseline to comply with Tier 2 apportionment
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: CDWR official accounting shows 2027 annual Colorado River diversions <4.3 MAF; structural compliance documented in USBR annual report

AMBER.reasoning: California dipping below 4.4 MAF QSA is a long-sacrosanct threshold crossing; captures legal-regime break.

BLUE.reasoning: CA compliance with QSA/Tier 2 is California institutional absorption of shortage through its existing water-rights hierarchy; textbook BLUE outcome.

RED.reasoning: CA cutting below 4.4 MAF QSA baseline under Tier 2 is the mandatory-compliance fire. Good.

VIOLET.reasoning: Names multiple specific decision-makers (agencies, officials, firms, states) and ties the outcome to their choices. Actor-incentive traceability strong.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF recovery above 1,075 ft (exits Tier 1), THEN Colorado River basin precipitation WY2027 reaches +35% to +50% above 30-year mean
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Observable: NRCS/USGS reports Oct 2026-Sep 2027 basin precipitation +35% to +50% vs 1981-2010 climatological mean

AMBER.reasoning: +35% to +50% basin precipitation is an extreme hydrological event; pairing it as the unique driver of recovery above 1,075 ft ignores management actions (demand cuts, ICS releases). 0.58 overstates the conditional given climate trend toward aridification.

BLUE.reasoning: Wet-year precipitation reaching +35-50% above mean is a natural recovery. BLUE-neutral.

RED.reasoning: +35-50% precipitation is extreme; narrow band misses that recovery above 1,075 could happen at +20-35% with demand cuts. Probability 0.58 reasonable but RED view: megadrought structural — recovery scenario much less likely.

GRAY.reasoning: Precipitation anomaly is directly measurable; 0.58 prob reflects low base rate correctly.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF Tier 2 range holds, THEN Arizona water agencies implement mandatory fallowing program reducing agricultural acreage 50-100 kAC in Pinal/Maricopa counties for 2028
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Observable: ADWR or CAP announces mandatory fallowing program; documented acreage reduction >=50 kAC executed in 2028

AMBER.reasoning: Mandatory fallowing program is a discrete institutional regime-shift signal.

BLUE.reasoning: Mandatory fallowing under Tier 2 is institutional mechanism working. BLUE-compatible.

RED.reasoning: Mandatory fallowing under Tier 2 is RED-aligned structural consequence.

GRAY.reasoning: Fallowing acreage is a strong structural observable.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H2 (Mead 1,050-1,075 ft, Tier 1 maintained), THEN basin-wide users consolidate adaptation infrastructure: groundwater-banking deposits, CAP-water purchase agreements, and agricultural-fallowing contracts lock in by end 2027 at >400 kAF annually. These institutional structures become sticky; even if Mead rebounds >1,075 ft in 2028, reverse-adaptation requires legislative action and faces entrenched stakeholder resistance. Structural dependency on groundwater persists.
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Observable: (1) ADWR, CAP, and other water authorities report 2027 cumulative: groundwater banking ≥200 kAF, CAP transfers/purchases ≥100 kAF, ag fallowing contracts ≥100 kAF. (2) These contracts are multi-year (2027-2030 commitments). (3) If Mead rebounds in 2028, formal review/sunsetting of adaptation contracts shows <30% voluntary early termination despite improved surface water.

AMBER.reasoning: Reverse-adaptation friction / groundwater-substitution lock-in is an excellent AMBER-style institutional path-dependence prediction.

BLUE.reasoning: Adaptation contract stickiness is institutional lock-in BLUE recognizes. Reasonable.

RED.reasoning: Groundwater-substitution institutional lock-in is sharp RED insight.

GRAY.reasoning: Contract-stickiness test (<30% voluntary early termination) is a well-formed reverse-adaptation observable.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H3 (Mead 1,025-1,050 ft, Tier 2), THEN agricultural transition becomes structural collapse: fallowing rates accelerate to 150-200 kAC by 2027-2028 in Pinal/Maricopa counties. Forced divestment of marginal farms (sub-50 acre operations) increases 40-60% by end 2028. Farmland value in affected counties declines 15-25% below 2024 baseline as capitalization of permanent water-scarcity expectations shifts property prices.
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Observable: (1) USDA FSA/satellite data shows 2028 irrigated acreage ≤-150 kAC vs 2024 baseline in Pinal/Maricopa. (2) Farm census or county tax assessor data shows farm-unit count declines 40-60% in affected region by 2028. (3) Agricultural land value per acre (county assessor records) declines 15-25% vs 2024 in same region.

AMBER.reasoning: Agricultural structural collapse with land-value capitalization is a clear regime-transition observable.

BLUE.reasoning: 15-25% farmland value decline assumes stuck-state with no institutional buffer. BLUE expects water markets, state purchase programs, and crop-switching (low-water crops) to partially clear. 0.69 too high.
BLUE.modified_suggestion: Lower to 0.50; consider water-market transactions softening price declines.

RED.reasoning: Structural agricultural collapse under Tier 2 is core RED-aligned outcome.

GRAY.reasoning: Farmland value + acreage + farm-count triangulation is robust.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF severe decline below 1025 ft holds, THEN >=1 state formally petitions federal water-rights modification outside Colorado Compact framework by 2027-12-31.
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Formal petition or legislation from state government requesting federal intervention to modify Colorado Compact allocation terms.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF elevation drops below 1025 feet by end 2027, THEN USGS records elevation at or below 1025 feet as of 2027-12-31 with Bureau of Reclamation citing inflow assumption failures.
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: USGS/Bureau of Reclamation publishes official Lake Mead elevation measurement at or below 1025 feet as of 2027-12-31. NOAA or USBR hydro forecast cites inflow assumptions exceeded downside scenarios.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF Lake Mead recovers above 1,075 ft by end-2027 (H1: exits Tier 1), THEN policy attention shifts from emergency management to long-term sustainability, with Interior Department and at least 3 state water agencies publishing multi-decadal climate-adaptation frameworks explicitly referencing post-shortage planning by Q1 2028.
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Tier 1 emergency measures formally expire; Interior Department and at least 3 state water agencies publish sustainability/long-term framework documents by Q1 2028 that explicitly reference post-shortage planning; federal appropriations for ecosystem restoration increase more than 25% YoY.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: Lake Mead recovery driving policy attention from emergency to sustainability is textbook mean-reversion. 48% appropriately modest.

Recovery above tier threshold triggers political pressure to relax restrictions, not invest in long-term frameworks. Water management institutions have repeatedly failed to institutionalize lessons from shortage periods once immediate pressure abates. 0.48 already cautious but appropriations criterion requires budget action in hostile environment.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Lower to 0.33. Reframe: recovery triggers rollback of conservation investments, not enhancement. Add: policy attention shift must be confirmed by actual appropriations bills passed, not merely agency publications.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: Recovery triggers political urgency reduction — bureaucratic actors after crisis passes are incentivized to declare victory and deprioritize long-term planning. Trump Interior Department has specifically rolled back Biden-era water sustainability frameworks.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Lower to 0.34. Named actor constraint: Trump Interior Department has demonstrated preference for deregulation, not multi-decadal federal climate-adaptation frameworks.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: Interior Department sustainability frameworks and state agency publications will materialize regardless of recovery (bureaucratically mandated by IRA and NRCS programs). 25% ecosystem restoration appropriations increase is the only meaningful distinguishing bar and may not follow from Lake Mead recovery alone.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Reduce to 0.38. Publication criterion is nearly certain regardless of H1 — it is bureaucratically mandated. Meaningful resolution is the 25% appropriations increase, which requires legislative action that post-crisis complacency disfavors.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: Resolution criteria (Interior + 3 state agencies publishing frameworks) describes normal bureaucratic activity that would occur regardless of Lake Mead elevation. 25% YoY ecosystem restoration appropriations increase is the only meaningful distinguishing bar.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Sharpen: require Interior Department formally lifts Tier 1 shortage designation AND at least 2 state legislatures pass updated water-use statutes explicitly referencing post-shortage conditions by Q3 2028.
